skill.name: arboreto
skill.description: Infer gene regulatory networks from expression data with Arboreto, GRNBoost2, GENIE3, and Dask-based scaling guidance.
skill.summary: Analyze transcriptomics data to infer transcription factor and target gene relationships.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static external-command and weak-cryptography alerts were reviewed and are mostly false positives from Markdown code fences, inline code, and ordinary words such as description. The skill is not malicious, but it includes a runnable Python script and distributed Dask examples that can read local files, write outputs, and connect to network schedulers. Publish with a warning about local filesystem access and securing Dask clusters.

medium_findings.title: Dask Cluster Networking Requires Security Controls
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for network exposure risk. The documentation shows remote Dask scheduler and worker connections plus a dashboard URL. This is legitimate distributed-computing guidance, but users could expose transcriptomics data or compute resources if they run these examples on an unsecured network.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints are explicit examples, and Dask cluster traffic can carry data and tasks. No malicious exfiltration intent was found, so the risk is operational rather than hostile.

low_findings.title: Runnable Script Uses User-Supplied File Paths
low_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for local filesystem interaction. The included script reads an expression matrix, optionally reads a transcription-factor file, and writes a network file using CLI arguments. This is expected for the skill purpose and does not invoke a shell, but users should run it only on intended files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The argparse inputs flow directly into pandas and output writing. The behavior is clear and legitimate, with no shell execution or hidden persistence.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for Ruby or shell backtick execution. The reported locations are Markdown code fences, inline references, Python examples, or visible commands such as package installation and Dask startup. No code path executes these strings automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation examples rather than Ruby backtick execution. The only shell commands are user-visible instructions, not hidden execution logic.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Substring Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for weak cryptographic algorithms. The cited lines contain scientific descriptions, headings, or argument text, not DES, MD5, SHA-1, or cryptographic API usage. No evidence found for cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact cited lines do not contain crypto algorithms or crypto calls. The scanner likely matched benign substrings inside normal prose or parameter descriptions.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Resource Monitoring Guidance
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for system or network reconnaissance. The cited text describes Dask worker configuration, monitoring, and cluster performance practices. No evidence found for host enumeration, scanning, or stealthy discovery behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is benign cluster sizing and scheduling advice. It does not instruct users to scan networks or collect host details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Distributed Scheduler Connection
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples connect clients and workers to Dask scheduler addresses. This is expected for scaling, but it should be limited to trusted, secured networks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scheduler addresses are explicit and semantically tied to distributed execution. The concern is exposure risk, not malicious behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Read and Write Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The script accepts input and output paths and processes local transcriptomics files. This is normal for the tool, but it can overwrite the chosen output path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are directly visible in the script. There is no hidden file traversal or command execution.

content.value_statement: Transcriptomics datasets are hard to turn into clear regulator and target relationships. This skill guides Arboreto workflows for GRNBoost2, GENIE3, TF filtering, reproducibility, and Dask scaling.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to load expression matrices for Arboreto analysis.
Guides GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 selection for gene regulatory inference.
Shows how to restrict inference to transcription factor lists.
Provides a runnable basic GRN inference script.
Documents local and distributed Dask execution patterns.
Describes output columns for transcription factor, target, and importance scores.
limitations: It does not validate biological correctness of inferred regulatory links.
It requires users to provide clean numeric expression data.
Distributed examples require users to secure their own Dask environment.
It does not include statistical significance testing implementation.

use_cases.title: Prioritize regulators in single-cell studies
use_cases.description: Infer candidate transcription factor and target relationships from cell-by-gene expression matrices.

use_cases.title: Compare regulatory networks across conditions
use_cases.description: Run consistent Arboreto workflows across control, treatment, and timepoint expression datasets.

use_cases.title: Scale GRN inference on clusters
use_cases.description: Use Dask clients and workers to process larger transcriptomics datasets beyond one workstation.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a basic GRN run
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one expression matrix.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Arboreto skill to plan a GRNBoost2 run for my expression_data.tsv file. Include input checks and expected output columns.

prompt_templates.title: Add transcription factor filtering
prompt_templates.scenario: Restrict inference to a known TF list.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Arboreto skill to adapt my workflow for a TF list named human_tfs.txt. Explain how the TF file should be formatted.

prompt_templates.title: Compare GRNBoost2 and GENIE3
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose an algorithm for a study design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Arboreto skill to compare GRNBoost2 and GENIE3 for my dataset size and validation goals. Recommend one workflow.

prompt_templates.title: Design a distributed inference workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Arboreto skill to design a Dask-based GRN inference workflow for 50,000 cells and 20,000 genes. Include security precautions.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to run GRNBoost2 on a tab-separated expression matrix.
output: A checklist for expression matrix shape, gene names, and numeric values.
A recommended GRNBoost2 workflow with a reproducible seed.
An explanation of the network output fields and how to inspect top links.

output_examples.input: A user asks whether to use GENIE3 or GRNBoost2 for a large single-cell dataset.
output: A recommendation to start with GRNBoost2 for speed and scale.
A note that GENIE3 can be useful for comparison or validation.
Practical advice about TF filtering and Dask resource planning.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to run Arboreto on a remote Dask cluster.
output: A high-level cluster workflow covering scheduler, workers, and client connection.
Guidance to use trusted networks and protect Dask endpoints.
Cleanup steps for closing clients and clusters after inference.

best_practices: Use GRNBoost2 first for large or time-sensitive transcriptomics analyses.
Set a random seed when comparing runs or documenting reproducible results.
Secure Dask scheduler and dashboard endpoints before distributed execution.
anti_patterns: Do not run distributed examples on public networks without access controls.
Do not infer networks from expression matrices with mislabeled gene columns.
Do not treat importance scores as validated regulatory proof without follow-up analysis.

faq.question: What data format does this skill expect?
faq.answer: It expects numeric expression data with observations as rows and genes as columns.

faq.question: Which algorithm should I try first?
faq.answer: Start with GRNBoost2 for most workflows because it is faster and designed for larger datasets.

faq.question: Can it use a custom transcription factor list?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show loading a TF file and passing those names to Arboreto.

faq.question: Does the skill prove biological regulation?
faq.answer: No. It infers candidate relationships that need biological interpretation and validation.

faq.question: Does it support cluster execution?
faq.answer: Yes. The references explain local Dask clients and remote scheduler connections.

faq.question: What security caution matters most?
faq.answer: Protect Dask scheduler and dashboard endpoints, especially when expression data is sensitive.
